repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
* 'shellcmdflag' states that its default value is set according to the value of 'shell', but this behavior is not yet implemented on Windows. The same applies to 'shellpipe', 'shellredir', and 'shellxquote'.
* On Windows, Git is often installed in paths containing spaces, and we still do not correctly resolve the sh executable name as described in 'shell'.
* On Windows, the default value of 'shellslash' is always `false`, which causes Unix-like shells to interpret `\` in paths returned by some functions as escape charaters.

## Solution
Use a simple rule table to detect common shells (e.g. `cmd`, `powershell`, shells whose names contain `csh` or `sh`) and apply sensible defaults, while leaving more complex scenarios to user configuration.

<!--
  Thank you for contributing to Neovim!
  If this is your first time, check out https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests-prs
  for our PR guidelines.
-->

try to close #31528, close #28384, close #16957

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -230,7 +230,7 @@ func SetShell(shell)
         if has("win32")
           " Nvim: default 'shell' is "sh" due to $SHELL being set in Makefile,
           " but here 'shell' should be cmd.exe.
-          set shell=cmd.exe
+          set shell=cmd.exe shellcmdflag=/s\ /c

comment_data.body: Because users expect `shellcmdflag` to be `-c` rather than `/s /c` on Windows when using a Unix-like shell as the default shell. That means `set shellcmdflag&` would reset it to `-c` in this case (because default 'shell' is "sh"). So when `set shell=cmd.exe` is used here, we need `set shellcmdflag` as well.

comment_data.body:
wasn't the old behavior (or at least the Vim behavior), that 'shellcmdflag' is auto-updated when the user sets 'shell'? 

I don't particularly like that "feature", but I think zeer is hinting that this change wouldn't be necessary if that behavior is preserved. And it may be required for back-compat (OTOH, shell options behavior has never been very good, so it's possible that users/plugins have such low expectations here that back-compat may not matter much).

comment_data.body:
> wasn't the old behavior (or at least the Vim behavior), that 'shellcmdflag' is auto-updated when the user sets 'shell'?

Eh, I tested this behavior while preparing the PR.  nvim/vim update these values and default values only once after reading the init file(i.e. `set_init_3`), and they are not auto-updated if 'shell' changes later.

As for this test case, I also compared it with [vim](https://github.com/vim/vim/blob/f1ed84158ab80240c42c9f2356767cad448ca151/src/testdir/test_plugin_glvs.vim#L144-L146). I guess it was originally intended to reset 'shell' to `cmd.exe` via `set shell&`, but due to the global `$SHELL` in the nvim test environment, `set shell&` effectively results in `shell=sh`. That's likely why `set shell=cmd.exe` is needed here. Based on this I thought adding `shellcmdflag=/s\ /c` would be reasonable.
However, I didn't dig into the exact purpose of the test itself–just assumed this change didn't end up making the test less meaningful. 😅

> And it may be required for back-compat

There shouldn't be any compatibility break here, because in the normal case where the default value of 'shell' is `cmd.exe`, the default value of `shellcmdflag` would still be `/s /c`, which is consistent with the previous behavior. 😂

comment_data.body:
Took me a second to realize this also covers "tcsh". But the help no longer explicity mentions that. that's confusing, we should still mention it

OTOH, I agree it's kind of redundant to list all the different "sh" shells, so maybe not.

comment_data.body:
does `invocation_path_tail` need a docstring update?

- Document that double-quoted segments are treated as a single token (spaces are not terminators inside quotes).
- Note the backslash-escape inside quotes (\", \\).
- what does `len` measure when quotes are present? (does it include the closing `"`?
- Give one short example, e.g. `invocation_path_tail("\"/usr/local/bin/foo bar/bash\" -c x", NULL) => "bash"`

AI claims there's a bug here, e.g. how does this behave with input:

      "/path/foo bar/bash"


> The only reason option.c's test passes despite this is that the new call site uses `len = NULL` and then does `strstr(p, "sh")`, which scans the full remainder of the string — so it finds "sh" inside "bash" even though `invocation_path_tail` itself terminated at the space. Any future caller that actually trusts `len` (or the null-terminated tail starting at the returned pointer) on a quoted invocation gets the wrong answer.

It's probably good to have an end to end test, but note also that `test/unit/os/shell_spec.lua` exists and can call the C functions directly, if needed.


however `test/functional/core/startup_spec.lua` might not be best for these shell tests. perhaps  a new `test/functional/options/shell_spec.lua` file is warranted, since the shell options behavior may get complex and `startup_spec.lua` is already quite big

comment_regular_data.body:
> Vim has some detection logic for this, but it was already lost in nvim's first commit

the logic can end up being quite a mess.

i wouldn't spend much time on this. or if we really do want it, i would move the logic to Lua, and call it via `nlua_call_excmd` or `nlua_call_vimfn`

> > Vim has some detection logic for this, but it was already lost in nvim's first commit
> 
> the logic can end up being quite a mess.

Totally agreed, I don't want to blindly port a patch here 😁. The commit above was just for doing a simple comparison test against Vim. What I'm actually trying to do is gather the potentially related issues and directions first, then think about what changes make sense to scope into this PR (Anything beyond that could be handled separately in other issues), e.g.,
1. I noticed you previously [mentioned](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/7846#issuecomment-357504968) that we probably shouldn't tokenize `p_sh`, and Bram also [noted](https://github.com/vim/vim/issues/459#issuecomment-361935381) that unfortunately extra flags currently have to live inside 'shell'. I assume this area could be improved in the future: A reasonable use case is users wanting to configure extra options for `:te`, such as `--login`, while `shellcmdflag` is more appropriate for non-interactive command flags.
2. You also [mentioned](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39762#issuecomment-4434111723) that we need to unify the job-control layer. Those related functions confused me before, and I assume this includes `tokenize/shellxquote/shellxescape...`, all that logic should live somewhere better. Or we could also ignore `shellquote/shellxquote/shellxescape...` logic for non-cmd shells as [mentioned here](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/28384#issuecomment-2060571893).
3. There's also the [Options refactor roadmap](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/25672), which I haven't looked into deeply yet. I've also briefly thought it'd be nice if these options could support `Array` in the future, so we wouldn't need to keep reparsing things back-and-forth.

> i wouldn't spend much time on this. or if we really do want it, i would move the logic to Lua, and call it via `nlua_call_excmd` or `nlua_call_vimfn`

I just checked the docs — those are for implementing an Ex cmd or f_xx function. The three issues linked above are mainly about adjusting shell-related options based on 'shell', and that logic currently lives in `set_init_3`. Would this be okay to live in Lua? Or do you mean moving `ex_bang` / `vim.fn.system` to Lua?

nice research!

> I just checked the docs — those are for implementing an Ex cmd or f_xx function

they are just a convenient way to sent typval structs to/from Lua. `nlua_call_vimfn` can be used by manually building a typval.

> How about replacing the whole condition logic in `set_init_3` with a small rule table?

Seems fine at a glance, but I would have to see the diff. Usually the logic doesn't end up fitting nicely in a table :)
